articles.title: Notes on using GNU Emacs' Tramp system in an unusual shell environment
articles.title_display: GNU Emacs Tramp Pitfalls in Unusual Shells
articles.summary: This post documents practical failures encountered while trying to use GNU Emacs Tramp over SSH in an unconventional shell environment. The author explains why Tramp depends on a conventional prompt, backslash quoting, and non-interactive login behavior, and why `sshx` can avoid some of those constraints by skipping the normal login shell. The most useful technical takeaway is that Tramp does not reliably respect a newly changed `tramp-remote-path` unless its persistent caches are fully cleared; in some cases, even restarting Emacs is not enough because Tramp stores host-specific path data on disk under `~/.emacs.d/tramp`. The author also notes that `tramp-own-remote-path` and `tramp-persistency-file-name` are harder to use than the documentation suggests, and recommends using `use-package` or `eval-after-load` to apply Tramp configuration after the package loads. The article is valuable as a troubleshooting note for Emacs users who need remote editing in nonstandard Unix setups, especially where PATH handling and shell startup files interfere with Tramp’s assumptions.

articles.title: Quoting Luke Curley
articles.title_display: WebRTC’s Low-Latency Bias Hurts Prompt Accuracy
articles.summary: This short quote argues that WebRTC is optimized for real-time audio, not reliable prompt delivery. It notes that WebRTC drops audio packets under poor network conditions to preserve latency, and says browser implementations make retransmission effectively impossible. The practical takeaway is that voice AI systems built on WebRTC may need to accept prompt degradation rather than wait for slower, more reliable delivery, which matters for anyone building low-latency conversational products.

articles.title: AI makes weak engineers less harmful
articles.title_display: AI Coding Agents Raise the Floor for Weak Engineers
articles.summary: The post argues that Claude Code and similar coding agents make weak engineers less harmful by preventing many obvious mistakes, such as bad cache keys, infinite loops, and resource leaks. It claims this shifts some low-performing engineers from being actively net-negative to producing work that is at least line-by-line plausible, though still often wrong in subtle ways. The author frames this as a practical change in team dynamics rather than a breakthrough in engineering quality. The main implication is that AI may reduce damage from weak contributors, but it can also slow their learning and turn them into thin wrappers around the model.

articles.title: I Will Not Add Query Strings to Your URLs
articles.title_display: Why Not to Add Query Strings to URLs
articles.summary: The author removed a referral query-string feature from Wander Console after it broke real pages and violated the principle of preserving user-intended URLs. A page that normally loaded fine, https://int10h.org/oldschool-pc-fonts/fontlist/, failed when altered to include a query string, returning a 404. The post argues that even seemingly harmless URL mutations can change or destroy resource identity, and that if a site wants attribution, the Referer header is the proper mechanism. The feature has been removed in release 0.6.0, with commit b26d77c documenting the fix. Practical takeaway: do not rewrite destination URLs for tracking, because small changes can break links and surprise both users and site owners.

articles.title: Using Claude Code: The Unreasonable Effectiveness of HTML
articles.title_display: Claude Code Case for HTML Over Markdown
articles.summary:
Simon Willison highlights a Claude Code team post arguing that HTML is often a better output format than Markdown for LLM-generated explanations. The practical payoff is richer artifacts: SVG diagrams, interactive widgets, inline annotations, navigation, and CSS/JS-driven layouts that can make complex answers easier to inspect than plain text. Willison says this changed his default assumption, especially for output generation.

He tests the idea on copy.fail, a Linux exploit writeup with an obfuscated Python proof of concept, by prompting GPT-5.5 to explain the code in detailed HTML. The resulting page is described as good, though it apparently focused too much on the Python harness rather than the exploit mechanics. The piece is mostly a prompt-engineering and format-selection note, but it has direct relevance for engineers using LLMs to generate reviews, explanations, and security analyses.

articles.title: George Orwell's review of Russel's Power: A New Social Analysis
articles.title_display: Orwell’s 1939 Warning on Power and Truth

articles.title: You gave me a u32. I gave you root. (io_uring ZCRX freelist LPE)
articles.title_display: io_uring ZCRX freelist bug leads to root
articles.summary: This writeup discloses a Linux kernel local privilege escalation in io_uring zero-copy receive (ZCRX): an out-of-bounds heap write reachable from a small integer bug in the freelist path, resulting in uid=0. The post targets Linux 6.15–6.19 before commit 770594e and requires CONFIG_IO_URING_ZCRX=y, so it is a concrete kernel exploit report rather than a generic vulnerability announcement. The title and metadata indicate the exploit chain is based on a freelist corruption primitive in the ZCRX receive path, with kernel heap impact strong enough to achieve root. For security engineers and kernel developers, the practical value is immediate: it narrows the affected versions and configuration, highlights a specific io_uring attack surface, and signals the need for patch review, backporting, and exposure assessment on systems using ZCRX.

articles.title: AI Is Breaking Two Vulnerability Cultures
articles.title_display: AI Is Changing Vulnerability Embargoes
articles.summary: The post argues that AI is weakening both major vulnerability-handling cultures: coordinated disclosure and Linux-style “fix it quietly” patching. In the Copy Fail case, a fix landed quickly, but another researcher spotted the implications and published a public write-up, ending the embargo within hours. The author’s point is that AI-assisted scanning makes it easier for outsiders to notice security fixes in commits, while also making defenders faster at validating and responding to issues. He notes that a 90-day disclosure window now looks increasingly unrealistic because multiple AI-assisted groups may independently find the same bug before the embargo expires. The post includes a small informal test with Gemini 3.1 Pro, ChatGPT-Thinking 5.5, and Claude Opus 4.7: all three identified a specific kernel commit as a security fix when given the commit itself, while performance dropped when only the diff was shown. The practical takeaway is that the old balance between secrecy and patch latency is shifting, and embargoes likely need to become much shorter.

articles.title: Apple, Intel have reached preliminary chip-making deal
articles.title_display: Apple and Intel Reportedly Discuss Preliminary Chip Deal
articles.summary: Reuters reports that Apple and Intel have reached a preliminary chip-making deal, according to the Wall Street Journal. The item is a market and supply-chain report rather than a technical disclosure, and it does not include implementation details, process-node data, packaging plans, performance numbers, or production timelines. For engineers, the main relevance is strategic: if true, the partnership could affect semiconductor sourcing and manufacturing capacity decisions across the PC and mobile ecosystem. The article provides no evidence beyond the reported talks, so it should be treated as early corporate news rather than a technical development.

articles.title_display: Cloudflare Blocked Cartoon Network Flash Games Page

articles.title: This Week on The Analog Antiquarian
articles.title_display: The Analog Antiquarian Pauses for Two Weeks

articles.title: Google Cloud Fraud Defence is just WEI repackaged
articles.title_display: Google Cloud Fraud Defense Repackages WEI-Style Attestation
articles.summary: Google Cloud Fraud Defense is presented as a reCAPTCHA evolution, but the article argues it is effectively Web Environment Integrity revived as a commercial product. Its core requirement is device attestation via Google Play Services / Play Integrity on modern Android or iPhone/iPad hardware, which excludes privacy-focused Android variants like GrapheneOS and LineageOS, and Firefox for Android. The author also argues the QR-code flow is easy for bot operators to bypass with cheap hardware and may train users into scanning malicious QR codes. More importantly, each successful challenge can create a persistent hardware-linked signal, turning fraud checks into a tracking mechanism across sites. The practical takeaway is that the product is framed as anti-bot defense, but its real impact is broader control and attribution over web access.

articles.title: Serving a Website on a Raspberry Pi Zero Running in RAM
articles.title_display: Serving a Website on a Pi Zero Entirely in RAM
articles.summary: This post walks through hosting a static website on a Raspberry Pi Zero v1.3 using Alpine Linux in diskless mode, with the root filesystem mounted from tmpfs/ramfs so the site runs entirely from RAM. The setup uses a 512MB Pi Zero, `lbu` to persist configuration on the microSD card, `darkhttpd` or `nginx` for web serving, and `rsync` for deployment. TLS is offloaded to a small VPS running HAProxy, with `socat` forwarding traffic from the VPS to the home network. The author also shows backup creation via a byte-for-byte `dd` image over SSH. Practical value is mainly for engineers interested in ultra-light self-hosting, diskless Linux, and low-resource deployment patterns.

articles.title: A web page that shows you everything the browser told it without asking
articles.title_display: Browser Fingerprinting Exposed as Interactive Web Story
articles.summary:
This interactive page demonstrates, in a concrete and readable way, how much a browser reveals on first load: IP-based geolocation, timezone, screen size, pixel ratio, language, GPU, installed fonts, battery status, storage quota, motion/orientation signals, referrer, and tab visibility changes. The source code shows the exact collection logic, including standard APIs such as WebGL debug renderer info, Battery API, Storage API, Visibility API, and font-width probing. It also states that only two anonymous beacons are sent to the site’s own server and that no cookies, localStorage, sessionStorage, IndexedDB, or service worker cache are used.

The technical value is mostly explanatory rather than novel research: it is a polished, source-visible demonstration of browser fingerprinting and passive tracking surfaces, with a strong emphasis on privacy implications. The page also includes practical implementation details like masking IPs in display, deterministic fingerprint-derived barcodes, and handling reduced-motion preferences. For engineers, the main takeaway is that modern browsers expose enough stable signals to identify users without cookies, and that many of these signals require no permission prompt. This makes the piece useful as a privacy awareness reference and as a compact catalog of browser-side tracking surfaces.

articles.title: Podman rootless containers and the Copy Fail exploit
articles.title_display: Copy Fail in Podman Rootless Containers
articles.summary:
This article tests CVE-2026-31431 (“Copy Fail”) against Podman rootless containers and shows that it can still obtain a container-root shell, but with a much narrower blast radius than a typical rootful Docker setup. The author reproduces the exploit in multiple configurations: rootless rootful, rootless non-root, with `--security-opt=no-new-privileges`, and with `--cap-drop=all`. In the non-root case, the exploit successfully escalates from user `foo` to container `root`, but `no-new-privileges` prevents the privilege change and keeps the process as `foo`. Dropping capabilities also blocks the practical escalation path.

The post explains the mechanics behind the results: Podman’s fork/exec model, user namespaces, `/etc/subuid` mappings, and Linux capabilities during image build and runtime. It also demonstrates how bind mounts behave under namespace remapping, including why host `root` becomes unmapped (`nobody:nogroup`) and how files created by the container map back to host UIDs. Beyond the CVE itself, the article recommends layered hardening: run non-root users in images, drop unnecessary capabilities, use read-only filesystems, limit binaries in the image, apply cgroups, and firewall container network access.

Practical takeaway: rootless Podman materially improves containment, but it is not a security boundary by itself; the kernel still needs patching, and container hardening significantly reduces post-compromise impact.

articles.title: Developing more confidence when tracking renames via Read­Directory­ChangesW
articles.title_display: Tracking renames more reliably with ReadDirectoryChangesExW
articles.summary: Switching from ReadDirectoryChangesW to ReadDirectoryChangesExW with ReadDirectoryNotifyExtendedInformation lets you inspect FILE_NOTIFY_EXTENDED_INFORMATION, which includes a FileId for each change. That FileId can be used to match FILE_ACTION_RENAMED_OLD_FILE and FILE_ACTION_RENAMED_NEW_FILE entries and verify they belong to the same rename operation. The post is a practical Windows filesystem tip, but it is a short implementation note rather than a deep investigation or benchmark-driven analysis.

articles.title: An Introduction to Meshtastic
articles.title_display: Meshtastic Introduces Off-Grid LoRa Mesh Messaging
articles.summary: Meshtastic’s introduction page describes an open-source, community-run system that turns inexpensive LoRa radios into an off-grid mesh messaging platform. The docs emphasize long range, no phone requirement for mesh communication, decentralized operation without a dedicated router, encrypted messaging, strong battery life, and optional GPS location features. It also notes a 331 km ground record and shows device connectivity across radios, Bluetooth, Wi‑Fi, USB, and the meshtastic-cli. For engineers, the practical value is a lightweight, license-friendly way to prototype resilient communications for remote or infrastructure-limited environments.

articles.title: GeoJSON
articles.title_display: GeoJSON Specification Overview

articles.title: A Runnable Reference Architecture for Battery Energy Storage Systems on InfluxDB 3
articles.title_display: InfluxDB 3 BESS Reference Architecture Goes Open Source
articles.summary: InfluxData published an open-source, runnable battery energy storage system (BESS) reference architecture for InfluxDB 3 Enterprise that can be started locally with Docker Compose in about two minutes. The stack is Python-first and includes a simulator generating roughly 2,000 points per second, a FastAPI + HTMX dashboard, scenarios for thermal runaway and cell drift, and database-native plugins for alerts and diagnostics. The article highlights three InfluxDB 3 features used in the design: Last Value Cache for sub-millisecond “current state” heatmaps over 768 cells, Distinct Value Cache for inventory and heartbeat queries, and the Processing Engine for Python triggers running inside the database. It also outlines how real BMS, PCS, and SCADA feeds would enter through Telegraf or OPC UA, with edge ingestion and replication to a central cluster. The practical value is a concrete, runnable blueprint for time-sensitive industrial telemetry systems, especially where high-cardinality sensor data and real-time safety logic matter.

articles.title: Mojo 1.0 Beta
articles.title_display: Mojo 1.0 Beta Launches With Python-Style Syntax
articles.summary: Mojo 1.0 beta is now available as 1.0.0b1, positioning the language as a Python-like, compiled, statically typed option for high-performance CPU and GPU programming. The announcement highlights Python interoperability, SIMD/vectorized kernel examples, compile-time metaprogramming, and a roadmap that still keeps core systems features and fuller Python compatibility in progress. The page also notes that the Mojo standard library is open source, while the compiler is planned to open-source in 2026. This is most relevant for engineers evaluating performance-oriented Python extensions or experimenting with GPU kernels in a single language, but it remains a product/positioning release rather than a deep technical report.

articles.title: Seth Michael Larson: Using Epilogue Retrace app with iPhone 13 Pro and Ubuntu
articles.title_display: Epilogue Retrace Fails on iPhone 13 Pro and Ubuntu
articles.summary: Seth Michael Larson reports that Epilogue’s Retrace app runs into two practical blockers: updating GB Operator firmware to v10.0.10 did not work from Linux Playback, and the firmware update ultimately required a Windows machine. He also found that a Lightning-to-USB-C adapter on an iPhone 13 Pro would not power the GB Operator, while a USB-C iPhone 15 Pro worked normally. The post is a useful compatibility note for anyone trying to archive Game Boy ROMs from Linux to iPhone with Epilogue hardware.

articles.title: Wander Console 0.6.0
articles.title_display: Wander Console 0.6.0 Removes URL Referral Parameters
articles.summary: Wander Console 0.6.0 removes support for the `via` referral query parameter after finding that some websites refuse to load when arbitrary query strings are appended. The author says the feature, added in 0.4.0 for access-log attribution, proved to be a misfeature even though it could be disabled in config. This release also includes minor UI adjustments and fixes, plus a changelog entry and commit reference (`b26d77c`) with more details. The practical takeaway is a small but useful design correction for a self-hosted, decentralized web console: favor compatibility over tracking convenience.
